refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain end-to-end teleoperation communication latency with p95 ≤ 150 ms under agreed network conditions to enable safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-008 addresses a critical safety and operational requirement. Teleoperation latency directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety margins. The p95 percentile metric ensures consistent performance under variable network conditions rather than optimistic average-case assumptions. High safety criticality demands explicit latency budgeting across the communication chain.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency (command input to sensor feedback display) under representative network conditions (agreed bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). Collect p95 percentile over extended operational sessions. Validate against 150 ms threshold under worst-case agreed conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode operator commands with minimal local processing latency.
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit encoded commands over the network link within latency budget.
FR-NGHE-008.3: The vehicle shall receive, decode, and execute remote commands with minimal processing latency.
FR-NGHE-008.4: The vehicle shall acquire, encode, and transmit telemetry feedback to the operator with minimal latency.
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall display received telemetry to the operator with minimal rendering latency.
FR-NGHE-008.6: The teleoperation system shall monitor and alert on latency degradation that approaches safety thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement a low-latency, deterministic teleoperation architecture with hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding, priority-queued network transmission, and real-time vehicle command execution; monitor latency continuously and degrade gracefully if thresholds are exceeded.
DP-B: Deploy a cloud-based teleoperation relay with edge computing nodes to minimize round-trip latency; use predictive buffering and command interpolation to mask network jitter.

DP-C: Implement a hybrid architecture with local command buffering and asynchronous telemetry streaming; use adaptive codec selection and network protocol optimization (UDP with custom reliability) to meet latency targets.

DP-D: Establish a latency budget allocation across subsystems (operator interface: 20 ms, network: 80 ms, vehicle processing: 30 ms, feedback rendering: 20 ms) and enforce via design constraints and real-time monitoring.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D (Latency Budget Allocation) combined with DP-A (Low-Latency Deterministic Architecture): Allocate the 150 ms p95 latency budget across functional subsystems (operator interface capture: 20 ms, network transmission: 80 ms, vehicle command processing: 30 ms, telemetry feedback rendering: 20 ms). Implement deterministic, hardware-accelerated encoding/decoding, priority-queued network transmission, and real-time vehicle execution. Enforce latency monitoring and graceful degradation when thresholds are approached.

potential_sources: Coupling between network link performance (DP-A network allocation) and vehicle processing capability (DP-A vehicle execution) is managed via explicit budget allocation and monitoring.
Coupling between operator interface responsiveness and telemetry feedback rendering is decoupled via asynchronous command/telemetry streams.
Safety alert generation (FR-NGHE-008.6) is decoupled from primary teleoperation path via independent monitoring.
coupling_check.notes: The selected DP avoids tight coupling by establishing clear latency budgets and independent monitoring. Network and vehicle subsystems can be optimized independently within their allocated budgets. Graceful degradation prevents cascading failures.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Network conditions remain within agreed parameters (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles). If network degrades beyond agreement, latency targets may not be achievable.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle hardware supports real-time command execution within 30 ms budget. Legacy or resource-constrained vehicles may require redesign.
RISK: Operator interface latency (20 ms) may be difficult to achieve on consumer-grade hardware; may require specialized input devices.
RISK: Telemetry feedback rendering (20 ms) assumes sufficient display refresh rate and GPU capability; may conflict with power/thermal constraints.
RISK: Network latency (80 ms) assumes direct, low-latency connectivity; satellite or heavily congested networks may violate this budget.
RISK: Graceful degradation strategy (reduced fidelity, command buffering) may degrade safety if not carefully designed.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly define latency budget allocation as a design constraint document. Establish network condition acceptance criteria (max RTT, jitter, packet loss) as part of system requirements. Define graceful degradation modes and their safety implications. Allocate contingency budget (e.g., 10–15 ms) for unexpected latency sources.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end latency budget allocation, monitoring, and enforcement. It encompasses operator command capture and encoding, network transmission scheduling, telemetry reception and decoding, feedback rendering, and latency monitoring/alerting. The module interfaces with the Vehicle (via Tasking and Telemetry) and Safety Services (via SafetyAlert) to enforce latency constraints.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module transmits encoded operator commands to Vehicle. Latency budget: 80 ms (network) + 30 ms (vehicle processing) = 110 ms from command encoding to execution.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module receives telemetry feedback from Vehicle. Latency budget: 20 ms (vehicle acquisition/encoding) + 80 ms (network) + 20 ms (rendering) = 120 ms from sensor acquisition to operator display.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module transmits latency degradation alerts to Safety Services when p95 latency approaches or exceeds 150 ms threshold. Enables safety-critical decision-making (e.g., operator override, command lockout).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Teleoperation module uses Network Link for bidirectional command/telemetry transmission. Owns scheduling, prioritization, and protocol optimization to meet 80 ms network latency budget.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of end-to-end latency management. All latency-critical functions (command encoding, network scheduling, telemetry rendering, monitoring) are cohesive and belong in this module. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces to Vehicle (Tasking/Telemetry) and Safety Services (SafetyAlert) are required.

Vehicle owns command execution and telemetry acquisition, but not the end-to-end latency budget or monitoring. Assigning latency management to Vehicle would scatter responsibility across operator interface, network, and vehicle subsystems. High diffusion risk.

Safety Services owns safety-critical decision-making (e.g., lockout on latency failure) but not latency measurement or enforcement. Assigning latency management to Safety Services would create tight coupling and violate separation of concerns. High diffusion risk.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container but is too coarse-grained for latency management. Assigning to NGHE Solution would prevent clear ownership and accountability. Latency is a Teleoperation-specific concern.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner of CR-NGHE-008 (Teleoperation latency). It owns the operator interface, command encoding, network transmission scheduling, telemetry decoding, feedback rendering, and latency monitoring. The module has clear external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) and can enforce latency budgets independently. Containment score: 95/100.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module may lack visibility into Vehicle command execution latency if Vehicle does not expose execution timing. Mitigation: Vehicle must report command receipt/execution timestamps in Telemetry.
RISK: Network Link performance is outside Teleoperation's direct control. Mitigation: Teleoperation monitors Network Link latency and alerts Safety Services if budget is exceeded.
RISK: Operator interface latency (20 ms) may be difficult to achieve on consumer hardware. Mitigation: Teleoperation specifies minimum hardware requirements for operator stations.

RISK: Graceful degradation modes (e.g., reduced telemetry fidelity) may be triggered by Teleoperation without Safety Services awareness. Mitigation: Teleoperation must notify Safety Services of all degradation events via SafetyAlert.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit interface contracts: (1) Vehicle must report command receipt and execution timestamps in Telemetry to enable latency attribution. (2) Network Link must expose latency metrics (RTT, jitter) to Teleoperation for monitoring. (3) SafetyAlert must define latency degradation severity levels and corresponding safety actions. (4) Teleoperation must define graceful degradation modes and their safety implications in a design document.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The selected DP (latency budget allocation + deterministic architecture) directly addresses the FR (end-to-end latency ≤ 150 ms p95). The DP is specific, measurable, and traceable to the FR. Latency budget allocation (20+80+30+20 = 150 ms) matches the acceptance criterion. Deterministic architecture and monitoring ensure consistent performance under agreed network conditions.

diffusion_risks: Latency monitoring and graceful degradation logic must remain cohesive within Teleoperation module. Risk: If monitoring is scattered across Vehicle and Safety Services, latency attribution becomes ambiguous.

Network Link performance is outside Teleoperation's control but critical to DP success. Risk: If Network Link is not optimized for low latency, DP cannot be achieved. Mitigation: Establish Network Link performance SLA as a system-level constraint.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module cleanly contains the DP. All latency-critical functions (encoding, scheduling, rendering, monitoring) are cohesive. External interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and minimal. No significant diffusion risk.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via end-to-end latency measurement under representative network conditions. Teleoperation module can instrument command/telemetry timestamps and compute p95 latency. Acceptance test: measure p95 latency over extended operational sessions and validate against 150 ms threshold.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation owns end-to-end latency management and enforcement. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.6) are allocated to specific subsystems (operator interface, network, vehicle, feedback rendering, monitoring). Each sub-FR has a clear owner and latency budget.

recommended_revisions: Establish a latency budget allocation document as a design constraint. Define network condition acceptance criteria (max RTT, jitter, packet loss) explicitly.
Define graceful degradation modes and their safety implications. Ensure Safety Services is notified of all degradation events.
Establish interface contracts: Vehicle must report execution timestamps; Network Link must expose latency metrics; SafetyAlert must define severity levels.
Allocate contingency budget (10–15 ms) for unexpected latency sources.
Define operator interface hardware requirements to achieve 20 ms command capture latency.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-008 is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs. The refined FR identifies six sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-008.1 through 008.6) that address specific latency sources: operator interface, network transmission, vehicle processing, telemetry feedback, and monitoring. Each sub-FR requires its own FR-DP-IM decomposition to establish detailed design constraints and ownership. The Teleoperation module assignment is sound and ready for sub-level decomposition.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-008.1: The teleoperation system shall acquire and encode operator commands with minimal local processing latency (budget: 20 ms).
FR-NGHE-008.2: The teleoperation system shall transmit encoded commands over the network link within latency budget (budget: 80 ms).
FR-NGHE-008.3: The vehicle shall receive, decode, and execute remote commands with minimal processing latency (budget: 30 ms).
FR-NGHE-008.4: The vehicle shall acquire, encode, and transmit telemetry feedback to the operator with minimal latency (budget: 20 ms).
FR-NGHE-008.5: The teleoperation system shall display received telemetry to the operator with minimal rendering latency (budget: 20 ms).
FR-NGHE-008.6: The teleoperation system shall monitor and alert on latency degradation that approaches safety thresholds (no explicit budget; continuous monitoring).

assumptions: Network conditions remain within agreed parameters (bandwidth, jitter, packet loss profiles).
Vehicle hardware supports real-time command execution within 30 ms budget.
Operator interface hardware can achieve 20 ms command capture latency.
Display hardware supports 20 ms feedback rendering latency.
Teleoperation module has visibility into Vehicle execution timing via Telemetry interface.
Safety Services is notified of all latency degradation events via SafetyAlert interface.
open_risks: Network latency (80 ms budget) may be difficult to achieve on satellite or heavily congested networks.
Operator interface latency (20 ms) may require specialized hardware; consumer-grade devices may not meet this target.
Graceful degradation modes may degrade safety if not carefully designed and validated.
Vehicle execution latency (30 ms) may be difficult to achieve on legacy or resource-constrained vehicles.
Telemetry feedback rendering (20 ms) may conflict with power/thermal constraints on operator stations.
evidence_needed: Network condition acceptance criteria document (max RTT, jitter, packet loss).
Latency budget allocation design document with contingency analysis.
Operator interface hardware specification and latency measurement data.
Vehicle command execution timing analysis and measurement data.
Graceful degradation mode specification and safety impact analysis.
End-to-end latency measurement data from prototype or simulation under representative network conditions.
Interface contract specifications (Vehicle Telemetry timestamps, Network Link metrics, SafetyAlert severity levels).
